rooms.question: If f(x) = x², what is f(-3)?
rooms.explanation: Substitute x = −3: f(−3) = (−3)² = (−3) × (−3) = 9. A negative number squared is always positive. The answer is not −9 — that would be −(3²), not (−3)².

rooms.question: What is the domain of f(x) = 1/x?
steps: \text{Domain = all x where f(x) is defined}
rooms.explanation: The domain is every x-value where the function produces a real output. Division by zero is undefined, so x = 0 must be excluded. All other real numbers (positive and negative) are fine.

rooms.question: Which graph represents a function? (Vertical Line Test)
rooms.latex: \text{Each } x \text{ maps to exactly one } y
steps: \text{Draw vertical lines — a function is crossed at most once each}
rooms.explanation: An upward parabola passes the vertical line test — every x has exactly one y. A circle fails it (two y-values for most x). A sideways parabola also fails. Two horizontal lines at different y-values give two outputs for their shared x.

rooms.question: What is the range of f(x) = x²?
rooms.explanation: x² is always ≥ 0: squaring any real number gives a non-negative result. At x = 0, f(0) = 0, so 0 is achieved — the range includes 0. It is not y > 0 (that excludes 0). It is not all reals (no negative outputs).

rooms.question: Order the steps to evaluate g(f(2)) where f(x)=x+1, g(x)=x²:
rooms.explanation: Composite functions evaluate inside-out. Identify f (inner) and g (outer) first. Then compute f(2) = 2+1 = 3, then apply g to that result: g(3) = 3² = 9. Working outside-in is the most common mistake.

questions.question: f(x) = √x. What is the domain?
questions.explanation: The square root of a negative number is not real, so x must be ≥ 0. x = 0 is allowed: √0 = 0. The domain is [0, ∞), not (0, ∞) — zero is included.

questions.question: g(x) = x³ − x. Is g even, odd, or neither?
questions.explanation: Test g(−x): g(−x) = (−x)³ − (−x) = −x³ + x = −(x³ − x) = −g(x). Since g(−x) = −g(x) for all x, g is odd. An even function would satisfy g(−x) = g(x), which doesn't hold here.

questions.question: h(x) = |x|. What is h(-5)?
questions.explanation: Absolute value returns the non-negative magnitude: |−5| = 5. It is not −5 (absolute values are never negative), not 0 (that's |0|), and not 25 (that would be (−5)²).
